title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections is threatened by unexpected sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing. These behavioral changes cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, potentially leading to hazardous situations and pedestrian-vehicle conflicts. The study aims to quantitatively model these sudden speed changes to improve safety assessment at signalized crosswalks.
method_summary: The study develops a pedestrian speed profile model using stepwise functions. A discrete choice model (multinomial logit) determines the probability of acceleration, deceleration, or constant speed at each time interval based on factors including necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec - Vcur), conflict area presence, and signal timing. Separate regression models are used for acceleration (linear regression) and deceleration (normal distribution). Monte Carlo simulation generates complete speed profiles. Data from video surveys at 5 crosswalks in Nagoya, Japan was used.
method_family: Discrete choice model with regression analysis
datasets: Video survey data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama, Ueda, Fushimi)
tasks: Pedestrian speed profile modeling, crossing time estimation, vehicle-pedestrian conflict simulation
metrics: Modified rho-squared (0.431), Modified R² (acceleration: 0.765, deceleration: 0.725), vehicle yield rate, Post Encroachment Time (PET)
baselines: Constant speed model (assuming pedestrians maintain their initial entrance speed throughout crossing)

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model represents pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model

The difference between necessary speed to complete crossing and current speed (Vnec - Vcur), conflict area dummy, and pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase have significant impacts on acceleration/deceleration choices
The pedestrian speed change model provides significantly higher vehicle yield rates compared to constant speed assumptions, demonstrating its importance for safety assessment
Different models are needed for periods before and after PFG onset due to significantly different speed change behaviors

limitations.text: The accuracy of individual speed-change event detection needs further improvement (23.6% error for non-acceleration, 43.8% for acceleration events)
Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction is not considered because pedestrian traffic volumes at study sites were relatively low
Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction (e.g., smartphone use) were not considered in the model
Limited sample size from only five sites prevented some expected variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from being significant
The model does not consider real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles

future_work: Improve accuracy of individual speed-change event detection
Incorporate pedestrian characteristics (age, gender) and distraction factors
Consider pedestrian-pedestrian interactions in high-demand conditions
Incorporate real-time interaction between pedestrians and vehicles
Use advanced data collection techniques like laser scanning for higher precision
Apply model to autonomous vehicle pedestrian detection and prediction systems
notes: The study was supported by the Memorandum of Understanding between Qatar Transportation and Traffic Safety Center at Qatar University and the International Center of Urban Safety Engineering at the University of Tokyo. Data was collected using the TrafficAnalyzer video image processing system.
